comments.text: Possible ambiguity about distinct denominators In Erdős Problem #282, the stated greedy algorithm appears to have a small ambiguity regarding distinct denominators. The problem text says to choose the smallest number n from the allowed set A such that n is at least 1 divided by x. Then it says to replace x by x minus 1 divided by n. It then says that if the process terminates, it gives a representation of x as a sum of distinct unit fractions. However, as written, the algorithm does not seem to exclude denominators that have already been used. For example, let A be the set of odd positive integers and take x = 2/3. The algorithm first chooses n = 3, giving 2/3 - 1/3 = 1/3. On the next step, the algorithm again chooses n = 3, giving 1/3 - 1/3 = 0. So the algorithm terminates, but it gives 2/3 = 1/3 + 1/3, where the denominator 3 is repeated. This is not a representation by distinct unit fractions. Therefore, either the algorithm should explicitly forbid previously used denominators, or the claim about distinct denominators should be weakened. I may be misunderstanding the intended convention, but I wanted to flag this possible ambiguity.

Well spotted, thanks! Although this is not made clear in [ErGr80], I assume that the 'obvious' greedy algorithm is what was intended - in other words, at each stage choose the largest unit fraction (with denominator from the targeted set) which has not been used so far.

Enumerate the set $A$ in the increasing order as $a(1),a(2),a(3),\ldots$. Quite obviously, the greedy algorithm converges but never terminates precisely for the numbers $x\in(0,\infty)$ in the set \begin{align*} G = \Big\{ \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{a(n_k)} : &\ n_k\in\mathbb{N},\ (n_k) \text{ strictly increasing}, \\ &\ \sum_{l=k+1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{a(n_l)} < \frac{1}{a(n_k-1)} - \frac{1}{a(n_k)} \text{ for } k\in\mathbb{N} \Big\}. \end{align*} (Here, $a(0)=0$, $1/0=\infty$.) Thus, for instance, the "question on squares" is equivalent to the possibility of writing a rational number $x\in(0,1)$ as\[ x=\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n_k^2}, \text{ where } \sum_{l=k+1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n_l^2} < \frac{2n_k-1}{(n_k-1)^2 n_k^2}. \]This "abstract nonsense" argument at least places the problem in the realm of irrationality questions. For instance, one can, rather cheaply, just apply the simple observation by Terry and myself under [263] to prove (non-constructively) that there exist $A$ asymptotically equal to the sequence of squares with a negative answer to the greedy algorithm termination. Moreover, one can even consider a Cantor-type set $G’\subset G$,\[ G' := \Big\{ \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{a(n_k)} : n_k\in[M_k,N_k] \text{ for } k\in\mathbb{N} \Big\} \]for carefully chosen $M_1,N_1,M_2,N_2,\ldots$, and try to find a rational number inside $G'$, which makes the problem look similar to [257]. In fact, this problem and many other irrationality problems would follow if we had a way of finding rational numbers in (sufficiently thick) Cantor-type sets. Of course, if the set $G’$ in question is "extremely" thick, then basic Diophantine approximations easily give (many) rational numbers inside $G'$. This can give a negative answer to the greedy algorithm termination also for some concrete $A$. In short, something could be done, but I am still undecided if this can help with any of the concrete questions stated above. Added note: Junnosuke Koizumi recently creatively used the connection between an irrationality problem and a greedy-type approximation.

The general problem certainly allows for very exotic behaviour to arise. Since Bloom determined which positive rationals are expressible as sum of distinct reciprocals of shifted primes, one particular question is whether the greedy algorithm always works in this situation.
